APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper fits the scope and audience of a top NLP/ML venue: it targets word representation learning, introduces a concrete extension (ngram contexts) applied across standard methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), and evaluates on widely used analogy and similarity benchmarks (Google, MSR, Levy et al.). From a reproducibility standpoint the authors provide code and hyperparameter reporting, but they note they did not tune ngram-specific hyperparameters and some methods worsen under defaults—an experimental shortcoming that lowers confidence but does not remove relevance. Overall the work is a solid, practically oriented contribution to embedding methodology with appropriate baselines and evaluations, warranting acceptance with minor experimental clarity/tuning improvements. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes an actionable modification to widely-used embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), evaluates on standard analogy and similarity benchmarks (Google, MSR, Levy et al.), and releases code. The framing—treating ngrams as contexts and a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction—targets the distributional semantics and representation-learning community and will interest researchers and practitioners. My only minor reservation is that the contribution feels incremental over prior subword/ngram embedding work and some methods (GloVe, SVD) show mixed results without hyperparameter tuning, but those concerns affect novelty more than venue fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper fits the scope and audience of a top NLP/ML venue: it targets word representation learning, introduces a concrete extension (ngram contexts) applied across standard methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), and evaluates on widely used analogy and similarity benchmarks (Google, MSR, Levy et al.). From a reproducibility standpoint the authors provide code and hyperparameter reporting, but they note they did not tune ngram-specific hyperparameters and some methods worsen under defaults—an experimental shortcoming that lowers confidence but does not remove relevance. Overall the work is a solid, practically oriented contribution to embedding methodology with appropriate baselines and evaluations, warranting acceptance with minor experimental clarity/tuning improvements.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues: it proposes an actionable modification to widely-used embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD), evaluates on standard analogy and similarity benchmarks (Google, MSR, Levy et al.), and releases code. The framing—treating ngrams as contexts and a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction—targets the distributional semantics and representation-learning community and will interest researchers and practitioners. My only minor reservation is that the contribution feels incremental over prior subword/ngram embedding work and some methods (GloVe, SVD) show mixed results without hyperparameter tuning, but those concerns affect novelty more than venue fit.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This paper tackles a meaningful problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngram co-occurrence—which yields measurable gains (notably on analogy tasks) and introduces a more memory-efficient construction for ngram co-occurrence matrices. From a reproducibility and practitioner standpoint the availability of code and application across four standard methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) make the idea practical, but mixed results (minor or worse outcomes for some methods), increased computational cost, and lack of thorough hyperparameter tuning limit immediate broad adoption. The work is a useful, actionable contribution that will likely prompt follow-up studies and targeted adoption rather than fundamentally change how embeddings are developed. | B(3): The paper addresses a relevant problem—improving word representations by explicitly incorporating ngrams—and demonstrates measurable benefits (notably on analogy tasks) across multiple embedding algorithms, which is practically useful. Its proposal to treat ngrams as contexts and the memory-efficient co-occurrence construction are likely to influence follow-up engineering choices, but the gains are mixed (small or negative for some models) and come with increased cost and limited hyperparameter exploration. Given these trade-offs and the existing body of ngram/subword work, the contribution is a solid, broadly usable increment rather than a transformative shift, opening sensible but not radical new directions.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles a meaningful problem—improving word representations by incorporating ngram co-occurrence—which yields measurable gains (notably on analogy tasks) and introduces a more memory-efficient construction for ngram co-occurrence matrices. From a reproducibility and practitioner standpoint the availability of code and application across four standard methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) make the idea practical, but mixed results (minor or worse outcomes for some methods), increased computational cost, and lack of thorough hyperparameter tuning limit immediate broad adoption. The work is a useful, actionable contribution that will likely prompt follow-up studies and targeted adoption rather than fundamentally change how embeddings are developed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a relevant problem—improving word representations by explicitly incorporating ngrams—and demonstrates measurable benefits (notably on analogy tasks) across multiple embedding algorithms, which is practically useful. Given these trade-offs and the existing body of ngram/subword work, the contribution is a solid, broadly usable increment rather than a transformative shift, opening sensible but not radical new directions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): From a reproducibility/engineering perspective this paper delivers a pragmatic, moderately novel twist—treating ngrams as context items and integrating them into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD is a useful, systematic extension but conceptually incremental over prior distributional methods. The memory-efficient construction of ngram co-occurrence matrices is a practical engineering contribution that meaningfully reduces hardware requirements and distinguishes the work. However, the core idea (augmenting contexts with ngrams) is a straightforward extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and limited hyperparameter tuning for ngram models suggests the empirical novelty is moderate rather than high. | B(3): The core idea—treating ngrams as additional context items and injecting their co-occurrence statistics into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD—is a coherent and useful extension but conceptually incremental relative to prior work on subword/phrase and n‑gram features (e.g., phrase modeling, character/subword embeddings). The paper’s value lies in systematically applying this perspective across multiple embedding algorithms and proposing a more memory‑efficient co‑occurrence matrix construction, which is a non‑trivial engineering contribution, but not a fundamentally new formulation. Given these facts, the contribution is moderate novelty: informative to practitioners and somewhat surprising in its cross‑method consistency, but not a breakthrough idea.

reviewer_a.justification: From a reproducibility/engineering perspective this paper delivers a pragmatic, moderately novel twist—treating ngrams as context items and integrating them into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD is a useful, systematic extension but conceptually incremental over prior distributional methods. The memory-efficient construction of ngram co-occurrence matrices is a practical engineering contribution that meaningfully reduces hardware requirements and distinguishes the work. However, the core idea (augmenting contexts with ngrams) is a straightforward extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, and limited hyperparameter tuning for ngram models suggests the empirical novelty is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_b.justification: The core idea—treating ngrams as additional context items and injecting their co-occurrence statistics into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD—is a coherent and useful extension but conceptually incremental relative to prior work on subword/phrase and n‑gram features (e.g., phrase modeling, character/subword embeddings). The paper’s value lies in systematically applying this perspective across multiple embedding algorithms and proposing a more memory‑efficient co‑occurrence matrix construction, which is a non‑trivial engineering contribution, but not a fundamentally new formulation. Given these facts, the contribution is moderate novelty: informative to practitioners and somewhat surprising in its cross‑method consistency, but not a breakthrough idea.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD across multiple standard similarity and analogy benchmarks and compares against appropriate baselines, which supports the central claims. However, critical sensitivity analyses are missing: the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and report that some models (GloVe/SVD) can worsen under default settings, and there is no mention of multiple runs, variance reporting, or controlled runtime/memory comparisons on the same hardware. Related work coverage is adequate, and limitations (computational/memory costs, limited benefit of ngram-ngram co-occurrence) are discussed, but the experimental rigor required for full reproducibility and to isolate contributions is incomplete. | B(4): The paper runs multiple embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) augmented with ngrams and evaluates across several standard similarity and analogy datasets (including Google and MSR analogies and datasets from Levy et al.), which provides solid empirical coverage to support claims about improved analogy performance. It reports hyperparameters, provides code, and discusses negative results (ngram–ngram co-occurrence being costly with little gain) and limits (no hyperparameter tuning for ngram variants), but it lacks thorough sensitivity/ablation analyses exploring hyperparameter effects and finer-grained contributions of different ngram types, so the experimental depth is strong but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates ngram-augmented SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD across multiple standard similarity and analogy benchmarks and compares against appropriate baselines, which supports the central claims. However, critical sensitivity analyses are missing: the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and report that some models (GloVe/SVD) can worsen under default settings, and there is no mention of multiple runs, variance reporting, or controlled runtime/memory comparisons on the same hardware. Related work coverage is adequate, and limitations (computational/memory costs, limited benefit of ngram-ngram co-occurrence) are discussed, but the experimental rigor required for full reproducibility and to isolate contributions is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs multiple embedding methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) augmented with ngrams and evaluates across several standard similarity and analogy datasets (including Google and MSR analogies and datasets from Levy et al.), which provides solid empirical coverage to support claims about improved analogy performance. It reports hyperparameters, provides code, and discusses negative results (ngram–ngram co-occurrence being costly with little gain) and limits (no hyperparameter tuning for ngram variants), but it lacks thorough sensitivity/ablation analyses exploring hyperparameter effects and finer-grained contributions of different ngram types, so the experimental depth is strong but not fully comprehensive.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear structure (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early (adding ngrams to four representation methods and a memory-efficient ngram co-occurrence matrix). Notation and methodology are presented consistently enough to reproduce experiments (hyperparameters are reported and code is available), and figures/tables/examples effectively convey results across similarity and analogy benchmarks. Minor clarity issues remain around the limited hyperparameter tuning for ngram models and some variable effects on GloVe/SVD that the authors note but do not fully dissect — a more explicit discussion or ablation there would improve reproducibility and reader guidance. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (introducing ngrams into four representation methods and a memory-efficient co-occurrence matrix). Notation appears consistent and related work and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables reporting analogy/similarity gains are informative; writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., some methods (GloVe/SVD) showing mixed results under default hyperparameters and the stated need for further hyperparameter exploration could be framed more clearly — but overall the exposition is strong and readable.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a clear structure (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early (adding ngrams to four representation methods and a memory-efficient ngram co-occurrence matrix). Notation and methodology are presented consistently enough to reproduce experiments (hyperparameters are reported and code is available), and figures/tables/examples effectively convey results across similarity and analogy benchmarks. Minor clarity issues remain around the limited hyperparameter tuning for ngram models and some variable effects on GloVe/SVD that the authors note but do not fully dissect — a more explicit discussion or ablation there would improve reproducibility and reader guidance.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (introducing ngrams into four representation methods and a memory-efficient co-occurrence matrix). Notation appears consistent and related work and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables reporting analogy/similarity gains are informative; writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain — e.g., some methods (GloVe/SVD) showing mixed results under default hyperparameters and the stated need for further hyperparameter exploration could be framed more clearly — but overall the exposition is strong and readable.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares its ngram-augmented variants against standard, appropriate baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) on common analogy and similarity datasets and reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics, which supports fair, reproducible comparison. The authors acknowledge when GloVe/SVD perform comparably or worse and discuss computational/memory trade-offs and implementation details (including a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction and publicly available code). However, they admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and the work appears to omit some closely relevant competing approaches (e.g., subword/character-aware methods like FastText) that would be natural baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares its ngram-augmented variants against the core, widely-used baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD) and evaluates on standard similarity and analogy datasets (including Google and MSR analogies and datasets from Levy et al.), which is appropriate. Comparisons appear mostly fair (same data and metrics, hyperparameters reported), but the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and note that GloVe and SVD sometimes fare worse under default settings—this leaves some risk of unfairness. The paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better and discusses computational trade-offs and a memory-efficient construction, though deeper analysis of why certain baselines degrade with ngrams is limited. Overall, coverage of relevant competing approaches is adequate, but a small set of additional tuning/ablation comparisons would make the evaluation comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its ngram-augmented variants against standard, appropriate baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, SVD) on common analogy and similarity datasets and reports hyperparameters and evaluation metrics, which supports fair, reproducible comparison. The authors acknowledge when GloVe/SVD perform comparably or worse and discuss computational/memory trade-offs and implementation details (including a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction and publicly available code). However, they admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and the work appears to omit some closely relevant competing approaches (e.g., subword/character-aware methods like FastText) that would be natural baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its ngram-augmented variants against the core, widely-used baselines (SGNS/word2vec, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD) and evaluates on standard similarity and analogy datasets (including Google and MSR analogies and datasets from Levy et al.), which is appropriate. Comparisons appear mostly fair (same data and metrics, hyperparameters reported), but the authors admit they did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and note that GloVe and SVD sometimes fare worse under default settings—this leaves some risk of unfairness. The paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better and discusses computational trade-offs and a memory-efficient construction, though deeper analysis of why certain baselines degrade with ngrams is limited. Overall, coverage of relevant competing approaches is adequate, but a small set of additional tuning/ablation comparisons would make the evaluation comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence that adding ngram-contexts improves word embeddings (notably on analogy tasks) across four methods and introduces a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction; code and hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. However, from a reproducibility and rigor perspective there are gaps: hyperparameters for ngram models were not tuned, GloVe/SVD sometimes worsen under defaults, and the summary does not report multiple runs or statistical significance/variance—limits that leave some uncertainty about robustness. Mathematical formulations appear consistent and the proposed memory-saving construction is plausible, but stronger ablations and controlled experiments isolating each change would be needed to consider the work technically flawless. | B(4): The paper’s central claim—that treating ngrams as context items and injecting ngram co-occurrence statistics into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD improves word representations—is backed by empirical results across multiple analogy and similarity datasets and by sensible methodological variants (word-predicts-ngram). Mathematical content appears straightforward and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary. However, the authors did not tune hyperparameters for the ngram-augmented models and do not report statistical significance testing, which leaves some uncertainty about the robustness of the gains and hyperparameter sensitivity. Overall the experimental evidence supports the main conclusions, with minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the paper’s claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence that adding ngram-contexts improves word embeddings (notably on analogy tasks) across four methods and introduces a memory-efficient co-occurrence construction; code and hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. However, from a reproducibility and rigor perspective there are gaps: hyperparameters for ngram models were not tuned, GloVe/SVD sometimes worsen under defaults, and the summary does not report multiple runs or statistical significance/variance—limits that leave some uncertainty about robustness. Mathematical formulations appear consistent and the proposed memory-saving construction is plausible, but stronger ablations and controlled experiments isolating each change would be needed to consider the work technically flawless.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s central claim—that treating ngrams as context items and injecting ngram co-occurrence statistics into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI and SVD improves word representations—is backed by empirical results across multiple analogy and similarity datasets and by sensible methodological variants (word-predicts-ngram). Mathematical content appears straightforward and there are no obvious derivation errors in the summary. However, the authors did not tune hyperparameters for the ngram-augmented models and do not report statistical significance testing, which leaves some uncertainty about the robustness of the gains and hyperparameter sensitivity. Overall the experimental evidence supports the main conclusions, with minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the paper’s claims.

main_claims: Introduce ngrams into four representation methods (SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD) to improve word representations.
Learning from ngram co-occurrence statistics yields improved word embeddings, with notable gains on analogy tasks.
Trained ngram embeddings reflect semantic meanings and syntactic patterns and are useful for tasks such as finding antonyms and collocations.
Propose a novel, more memory-efficient way to build ngram co-occurrence matrices to reduce hardware requirements.
_summary.methodology: Treat ngrams as context items by adding ngram entries to the context vocabulary (word-predicts-ngram variant) and incorporate ngram co-occurrence statistics into SGNS, GloVe, PPMI, and SVD; evaluate resulting embeddings on word similarity and analogy tasks.
datasets: Six similarity datasets and two analogy datasets from Levy et al. (2015)
metrics: Analogy task accuracy using additive (add) and multiplicative (mul) evaluation functions
limitations: Introducing ngrams increases computational and memory costs (ngram-ngram co-occurrence yields little improvement but high cost).
GloVe and SVD show only minor or sometimes worse results when adding ngrams under default hyperparameter settings.
The authors did not tune hyperparameters for ngram models and state that relationships between ngrams and hyperparameters require further exploration.

reviewer_a.name: Practical Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating representation learning systems for NLP in both academic and production settings.
traits: looks for thorough baselines, clear ablation studies, and controlled experiments that isolate the contribution of each change
values precise experimental details (hyperparameters, data preprocessing, vocabulary/pruning, negative sampling, training time, and hardware) to enable reproduction
checks for appropriate statistical treatment (multiple runs, variance reporting, significance testing) and robustness across datasets
evaluates computational/ memory trade-offs practically (wall-clock, peak memory, complexity) and expects comparisons measured on the same footing
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who emphasizes experimental rigor, reproducibility, and clear ablations to support empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: NLP Representation & Impact Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor and long-time researcher in distributional semantics and word representation learning, with a track record of work on subword/phrase representations and evaluation benchmarks.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior ngram/character/subword embedding work and how this paper advances conceptual understanding
values evaluation choices that demonstrate real impact on downstream tasks beyond intrinsic metrics and interpretable analyses of learned representations
notices whether the proposed ideas address important gaps (e.g., semantics vs. syntax, rare-word handling, antonymy/collocation modeling) and their broader applicability
considers theoretical motivation and the clarity of framing (why treating ngrams as contexts is the right abstraction) as well as limitations and ethical/usage implications
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Big-picture, impact-focused reviewer who weighs novelty and practical significance in the context of the field and suggests directions to increase real-world relevance.
